Jeff,

Great points. I think that folks have just gotten used to such incredibly high scores that when a producer that they like is used to getting 95+ scores and gets a 91, something is ‘not right’.

That said, I tend to believe reviewers do look at what other reviewers give the same wine - and are probably hesitant to go very far afoot. Has SQN ever received a score below 90 on ANY of their wines from ANY reviewer? Curious . . .

The other thing that everyone here needs to understand that the setting and manner in which these reviews are conducted may not be ‘identical’. Perhaps one reviewer is doing tastings at home and only tasting X number of wines, whereas another reviewer is tasting wines in another setting and tasting XX number of wines per setting. This most likely will affect results. And what about timing of tastings? In some cases, there may be months difference in when the wines are being tasted - and this certainly can lead to different results.

Cheers.

I feel like the critics are damned if they do and damned if they don’t. People complain when certain critics dare to give out several 100s, 99s, 98s, but when they go to 90 or god forbid the dreaded 80s, people complain again. While it’s very possible that the same people aren’t complaining about both ends of the spectrum, it seems like people want critics to use a 90 - 96 point scale. Furthermore, sometimes it seems as if people are looking for validation of their tastes/preferences more than information to help them better understand a wine/vintage/producer.

I really appreciate when critics are willing to give their honest opinion of a wine, even if they really disliked it, whether I agree with them or not. It’s another useful datapoint for me.

When I said I appreciate his honesty, I more meant I appreciate his ability to seemingly score and review without suffering from an overwhelming affect of bias or cognitive dissonance. While I don’t think any other reviewers are being dishonest, at times, I feel scores may just a bit suspect when they align nearly perfectly with the price of the wine, especially when the wines weren’t tasted blind. I think a lot of, if not most, people (both professional and casual) have trouble completely separating the price and reputation of a wine/producer from their thoughts on the wine. This would also be supported with basic principles of psychology, and often seems potentially evident in many pro publications as well as on CellarTracker and Vivino.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Out of curiosity I checked a few of the really low Galloni scores against WA reviews. Galloni gave the Rudd Oakville Estate an 85. LPB gave it a 98+. That is a crazy difference in reviews. [I don’t think I have had one of their wines in a decade so I don’t really have an opinion one way or the other on which of those reviews is accurate, though I tend to find Galloni’s reviews more helpful.]

The [2018] Rudd Estate reviews are an interesting example. Here’s Antonio’s note:

“The 2018 Red Oakville Estate is similar to the Samantha’s in this vintage in that it lacks fruit as well as body. An aggressive finish does not help.”

Here’s Lisa’s:

“Deep garnet-purple colored, it sashays out of the glass with flamboyant scents of candied violets, red roses, black raspberries and fragrant earth over a core of cassis, wild blueberries and Morello cherries with a waft of pencil lead. Medium to full-bodied, the palate is an exercise in poise, featuring beautifully balanced velvety tannins and seamless freshness framing the opulent black and red berry layers, finishing very long and perfumed.”

There is no way that these are comparable bottles. This goes way beyond differences in palate. AG’s “lacks fruit as well as body.” LPB’s has flamboyant fruit and an opulent body. There’s just no way trained tasters could be that diametrically opposite unless the wines they tasted were diametrically opposite.

There’s just no way trained tasters could be that diametrically opposite unless the wines they tasted were diametrically opposite.

Except Galloni basically hated the entire lineup, so it’s hard to chalk it up to bottle variation. Either something was up with his tasting (palate fatigue, whatever he ate for lunch clashing with bigger/Napa wines—hard to imagine he’d make that mistake, etc) or it’s a not about the wine (i.e. LBP didn’t want to put herself out there and pan a $300 wine).

As I said, it goes beyond liking or disliking. They simply aren’t describing the same thing. All of AG’s reviews of the 2018 and 2019 Rudd’s have a very similar description: no fruit, no depth, abrupt finish. (Which made me wonder how a cab with no fruit and no body got a score of 85; in my book, that’s a 50 or so, unless it’s 100 years old.) Since that isn’t normal for boutique California cabs – or his past reviews of Rudd, which are quite positive --, the likeliest explanation is his bottles were flawed.

As Brian pointed out above, we have thousands of reviews to compare, and AG’s and LPB’s reviews are generally quite consistent.

Hard to believe that these two reviewers are describing the same wines, tasted within the same general timeframe, especially considering how strongly correlated their scores tend to be.

“Deep garnet-purple colored, the 2019 Nomad Cabernet Sauvignon Beckstoffer Dr. Crane explodes with blackcurrant cordial, fruitcake and blueberry preserves scents, plus hints of cast-iron pan, black truffles and tree bark. Full-bodied, concentrated and oh-so velvety, it has an electric line of freshness supporting the many layers of rich, hedonic fruit, finishing very long and earthy.” 98 points

“The 2019 Cabernet Sauvignon Nomad Beckstoffer’s Dr. Crane Vineyard is overripe and pruney, but also not as harsh as some of the other wines in this range. Inky dark fruit, chocolate and espresso are all kicked up here.” 81 points

I can’t speak to that particular wine, but this contrast resonates with me. I seem to be much more sensitive to some of these things than most people, so “harsh”, “pruney”, “raisiny”, “green extraction” can stand out and ruin a rich, hedonistic wine for me. The important thing to keep in mind is these characteristics tend not to age out. Big fruit mellows, and those underlying unpleasantries often become prominent down the road. So, take it as a red flag. For those who like the wine, buy to drink on the young side, within a certain time window. Age it too long, and you might regret it.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
I’ve just read through this thread and it is a hilarious microcosm of the larger Wine Forum re scores, critics, favorites, excuses, stylistic preferences, experience v. professional criticism, etc. A fun ride.

I have a few thoughts. First, there is absolutely a prevalent style in Napa. It may not be as big and jammy as it was, but it’s 14.4-14.9% abv, copious new oak (like 60-100% new french), in a fruit dominant but structured style. Some go bigger. Some go a little bit leaner. But this idea that there are so many exceptions to the norm is probably true in number of wineries and number of pieces of marketing material that suggest that, but it certainly has not been my experience drinking high end Napa cabs over the past 15 years. There has certainly been a correction AT CERTAIN WINERIES that used to be over the top and are now a bit more restrained, but “bit more restrained” still has little in common with wines being produced in 1991, and probably more in common with big bombers from the late 2000s than the wines from 1991. This thread debate reminds me of when people list off traditional napa cabs and mention Forman without somehow acknowledging that for years they’ve been producing 15% abv bombers.

Second, I would like to see Galloni run a second round blind tasting in which half of the bottles are second reviews of wines he panned, and half are bottles that scored well during his first review. If he has the same opinion on a second go around in a semi-blind setting, then keep em panned. If he changes his mind, let it be known. I don’t know about you guys, but I’ve had days where I just suck butt at tasting. Can’t smell well, can’t taste well. Three days later I might be spot on. I still remember being on a trip with my dad 11 years ago and on day 1 I could just not get anything from the wines we drank. He was gushing and I was just not on. Great wines, perfect condition, and I couldn’t get much at all. Change of location, temperature, allergens, you name it. Day 3 I was back and it was fun again. Maybe a root day and a fruit day? Lol. Or hell, maybe Galloni and I brushed our teeth too close to tasting appointments that day, or maybe we burned our mouth on a cafe americano.

Third, I think it’s great when critics hammer a bad wine. It’s good for accountability. If it’s over the top, kill it and say so. If it’s a gross underperformer, kill it and say so. You have paying customers who want your honest opinion. You have an ethical obligation to provide it. I own some Alpha Omega wines, and I am willing to believe that Galloni’s note is spot on for the 2019 ERA. I’m trying to get off their list. They’re 15+ % abv oak bombs. Sometimes their Beckstoffer To Kalon and Las Piedras can have a line of acidity and verve that balances out the size, but ERA is like everything to 11.

That is one of the worst issues with smoke taint. The molecules that are bound up within the wine unbinds slowly over time, and wines that appear fine, show the smoke taint later on. This can take up to 3-4 years to happen.

And while there is testing that can be done, the compounds tested for do not correllate with perception of smoke in the wine 100% of the time(or anywhere close to 100%).

The [2018] Rudd Estate reviews are an interesting example. Here’s Antonio’s note:

“The 2018 Red Oakville Estate is similar to the Samantha’s in this vintage in that it lacks fruit as well as body. An aggressive finish does not help.”

Here’s Lisa’s:

“Deep garnet-purple colored, it sashays out of the glass with flamboyant scents of candied violets, red roses, black raspberries and fragrant earth over a core of cassis, wild blueberries and Morello cherries with a waft of pencil lead. Medium to full-bodied, the palate is an exercise in poise, featuring beautifully balanced velvety tannins and seamless freshness framing the opulent black and red berry layers, finishing very long and perfumed.”

There is no way that these are comparable bottles. This goes way beyond differences in palate. AG’s “lacks fruit as well as body.” LPB’s has flamboyant fruit and an opulent body. There’s just no way trained tasters could be that diametrically opposite unless the wines they tasted were diametrically opposite.

One of these two critics is currently unemployed…

FWIW, I struggled continuously with Lisa’s reviews. I found her inconsistent, and never included context. She also never included anything interesting that might have changed at the winery from year to year either.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
I’ve just read through this thread and it is a hilarious microcosm of the larger Wine Forum re scores, critics, favorites, excuses, stylistic preferences, experience v. professional criticism, etc. A fun ride.

I have a few thoughts. First, there is absolutely a prevalent style in Napa. It may not be as big and jammy as it was, but it’s 14.4-14.9% abv, copious new oak (like 60-100% new french), in a fruit dominant but structured style. Some go bigger. Some go a little bit leaner. But this idea that there are so many exceptions to the norm is probably true in number of wineries and number of pieces of marketing material that suggest that, but it certainly has not been my experience drinking high end Napa cabs over the past 15 years. There has certainly been a correction AT CERTAIN WINERIES that used to be over the top and are now a bit more restrained, but “bit more restrained” still has little in common with wines being produced in 1991, and probably more in common with big bombers from the late 2000s than the wines from 1991. This thread debate reminds me of when people list off traditional napa cabs and mention Forman without somehow acknowledging that for years they’ve been producing 15% abv bombers.

Second, I would like to see Galloni run a second round blind tasting in which half of the bottles are second reviews of wines he panned, and half are bottles that scored well during his first review. If he has the same opinion on a second go around in a semi-blind setting, then keep em panned. If he changes his mind, let it be known.

So, the prevalent style of all Napa Cabs is the same as the prevalent style of “high end” Napa Cabs? Isn’t it true the old school-style producers tend to get much lower scores on their wines? That “high end” is defined by self-promotion and high scores and all that? We aren’t that far past an era where the quality of Napa Cab was pretty much the domain of one critic, James Laube. How often did he rate a classic styled wine above 92? Why would any producer who wanted to make that style create a business model that depended on scores? And, of course, it would be very difficult for them to charge outrageous prices without the critical buzz. But, if that’s your preferred style, perhaps you like a (now) $80-120 wine over some $500+ extreme IPA wine equivalent or $250 blueberry milkshake. Not “high end”?

On point#2, I can’t speak for all publications, but some notify the winery of the rating enough before publication that they have a chance to re-submit. So, there is a sort of appeals process. Also, some request two bottles of everything (though it sounds like some Napa stuff is done via small sample bottles?), so critics can investigate any apparent anomaly.

I’m struggling to follow your response about “prevalent style.” I don’t really get your point or points. There is obviously a disparity in style between high end and low end purely on account of cost. If I sell at 12 dollars a bottle, I’m not likely going to have spectacular grapes and new french oak to play with. Roy Piper released an excel spreadsheet showing his costs and expenses for making his really nice cabs. It costs a lot to make good wine. If my production is so small I make one barrel, I’m going to have less of an ability to consistently blend to a house targeted style. But if we’re talking about well made, high quality wines, I absolutely think there is a prevalent style, whether $80 or $150 or $250. There may be a gradation in quality, but quality and style are two different things, assuming a relatively level playing field. Obviously I’m not comparing Apothic Red to Screaming Eagle…If I wanted to compare cheap wines with residual sugar, I’d compare Apothic to Caymus.

I don’t think my self-defined prevalent style is limited to high end. I think it’s prevalent for most high quality producers though. Even so I’ve clearly built in a range and recognize that there is a sliding scale within the prevalent style. That should be expected. It would be like saying men’s suits are trimmer fit now than they were in 1995, and allowing that while some pant lengths, pant legs, and jackets may be shorter or tighter than others, generally speaking, the prevalent style is a much trimmer fit. That variation within the prevalent style does not contradict the fact that there is very obviously a prevalent style for men’s suits that is much trimmer than that in 1995.

Do old school style producers tend to get much lower scores on their wines? Tough to say. Kapscandy has made some 13%ers over the years and some 14.1s that got extremely high scores. Their richer wines have too. Which producers are you talking about? Ridge Monte Bello and Dominus pretty much always got monster scores, even as their styles got slightly more modern. Screaming Eagle is way more traditional in flavor profile, with its bright red cherry fruit, than Hundred Acre with its black currant, plum, and date. Even so, it gets great scores. If someone rolled out a 12.5% abv Napa cab with big tannins and high acid, I think it would get panned. No one is making that wine in Bordeaux anymore either. Even so, there has been a contraction in size. At the end of last decade, my range probably would have been 14.7-15.4% abv as the norm.

Re Laube…when was Laube god of Napa? Late 80s through mid-90s? After that Robert Parker was much more influential. Laube didn’t give much of anything over a 92 during the height of his reign, and 92 point wines were typically sensational. He lost credibility and following as his scores crept up and he matriculated to higher octane wines. I think that Laube may have been the man now if he’d remained Allan Meadows-esque in his scores. A Laube 92 certainly meant more than a Parker 92, just like a Suckling 94 for a burg means less than a Meadows 94.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
But, as I often point out, about 3/4 of wines in our blind tasting group get both first and last place votes. Some of those wines have great love-hate disparities that would equate to 25 points or more.

I do often wonder how tasters like Galloni and Laube and Parker and LPB and others who taste 200 wines in a day or 50 2oz pours in a sitting can really give a consistent assessment of quality in blind settings. At even 20 wine blind events I’ve participated in, a wine that is clearly not the highest quality can sometimes win “best of” simply because it is different than the other wines. I saw a 2007 napa cab blind tasting play out that way. 19 brings that were all within a similar profile of fruit, complexity, smoothness, structure. And one stylistic outlier that was more rough hewn. Alone it was not that exciting and not of particularly high quality. But that made it unique and so different and interesting. It was the speed bump on a long straightaway.

On the flip side, can’t really want critics to taste non-blind like many do. Confirmation bias, brand bias, etc. are all extremely real. Even more frequently at blind tastings an outrageously expensive wine is second to a lesser priced wine. After the reveal, the 3x more expensive wine magically begins to “open up and really strut its stuff with air,” eventually rallying to “now that it’s opened up, this is my WOTN.”

posts.cooked:
…
What do critics get by constantly stroking high end wines?

Readers, employment, access. For a start.

With the Cali Cult Circle Jerk among producers, wholesalers, retailers, auction houses and wine writers, consumers may think they are buyers, but they are really the product. That wine writer you love so much? You are the product that he/she is selling to the others in the chain of commerce.

Wine writers are all trading in points. More points = more revenue. Just ask Suckling. Vroom Vroom.

This is the hard part. Score inflation drives viewership and sales. Sticking to a more conservative scoring range generally reduces the audience to experienced buyers which is a pretty small slice of the pie.

Peter,

Wow - let me know how you really feel . . .

I have and will continue to voice my opinions about all things wine - even when they don’t follow what others believe. I remember ‘applauding’ Asimov when he posted an article on CA syrahs because, at the time, NO ONE was talking about CA syrahs, including on that board. Did I agree with Asimov’s sentiments? Nope - but I was happy to see the spotlight on a variety that, at that time, was receiving little. I took so much crap from folks at that time for doing so - but I did continue to post after that . . . and I have continued to post here, even when folks may ‘disagree’ with what I have to say.

Did I disagree with Parker about the presence of Brett in Pegau? Yep - and I still stand by that. We had different bottles - and we have different perspectives.

And yep, I CONTINUE to jump in whenever there is a thread about SB County wines to this day because, well, there’s still not a lot of buzz about our region at this time. I love making wine from where I do - and love the region as a whole and want to see more folks come here and explore it. Do I invite folks to visit? Sure - if they are coming to the area . . . why not?

And I stopped posting because I had pretty much ‘moved’ over here - especially when traffic nearly came to a grinding halt and most of those I enjoyed bantering with were no longer there.

Cheers.
